summary: Joel argues that the law of leaky abstractions has caused the amount of knowledge required to be a proficient programmer to expand enormously over time. While you can learn 90% of a programming platform in a week, the remaining 10% takes years to master, and that's where experienced programmers truly differentiate themselves. He catalogs the vast 'worlds' of programming knowledge (Windows, Java, Linux, Mac) and argues that no one can master more than one or two. The post criticizes people who dismiss other platforms from ignorance, and examines cross-platform GUI solutions like Java AWT, XUL, SWT, and wxWindows. Joel concludes that teams should always include architects with deep platform experience and should choose platforms based on existing team expertise rather than trendiness.
key_points: The hockey stick learning curve means 90% of daily programming knowledge comes quickly, but the critical remaining 10% takes years of accumulated experience
Programming 'worlds' (Windows, Java, Linux, Mac) are so vast that real proficiency in even one takes years, and very few people master more than two
The ratio has reversed: most programming today is calling APIs rather than writing low-level algorithms, making platform-specific knowledge more important than raw coding skill
Cross-platform GUI frameworks (Java AWT, XUL, SWT, wxWindows) each represent enormous worlds that require years of experience to truly evaluate
Flame wars between platform advocates are pointless because people who only know one world mistake their own familiarity for that world's simplicity
money_quotes: The sheer amount of stuff you have to know to be a great programmer is expanding all the time.

Becoming proficient, really proficient, in just one programming world takes years. Sure, lots of bright teenagers learn Delphi one week and Python the next week and Perl the next week and think they are proficient. Yet they don't have the foggiest clue how much they're missing.
You can learn 90% of what you use day by day with a week of learning. But the other 10% might take you a couple of years catching up.
Someone who is a fantastic C++ coder with no API experience only knows about 10% of what you use every day writing code that runs on an API.
People who only know one world get really smarmy, and every time they hear about the complications in the other world, it makes them think that their world doesn't have complications.

The software worlds are so huge and complicated and multifaceted that when I see otherwise intelligent people writing blog entries saying something vacuous like 'Microsoft is bad at operating systems,' frankly, they just look dumb.
You have to sweat and bleed with the thing for a year or two before you really know it's good enough or realize that no matter how hard you try you can't make your UI taste like real food.
Don't start a new project without at least one architect with several years of solid experience in the language, classes, APIs, and platforms you're building on.
key_insight: Platform-specific knowledge now dwarfs raw programming ability in importance, and the vast complexity of each programming 'world' means teams must prioritize deep experience over theoretical versatility.
